title: Arlington National Cemetery Tour
excerpt: Discover history, honor heroes, and experience solemn traditions on an Arlington National Cemetery tour, a meaningful visit for all ages.
seoTitle: Arlington National Cemetery Tour - Landmark Educational Tours
seoDescription: Discover history, honor heroes, and experience solemn traditions on an Arlington National Cemetery tour, a meaningful visit for all ages.
html:
Exploring Arlington National Cemetery: A Powerful Journey Through American History

An Arlington National Cemetery tour offers students and visitors a profound journey into American history, sacrifice, and remembrance. This renowned cemetery, spanning over 600 acres of rolling Virginia hills just across the Potomac River from Washington, D.C., serves as the final resting place for more than 400,000 military veterans and significant historical figures. More than just a burial ground, Arlington is a living tribute that connects visitors deeply with America’s past and present.

Arlington National Cemetery Tour

History of Arlington National Cemetery

Arlington National Cemetery was officially established during the Civil War on the grounds of Arlington House, once the home of Confederate General Robert E. Lee. As casualties from the war mounted, the U.S. government transformed the grounds into a military cemetery in 1864. Today, Arlington stands as a solemn reminder of America’s sacrifices, conflicts, and the enduring spirit of service and honor.

What to Expect on an Arlington National Cemetery Tour

Tomb of the Unknown Soldier

One of Arlington’s most visited sites is the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier, a powerful memorial dedicated to unidentified American service members who gave their lives in combat. The tomb, guarded 24 hours a day by the elite soldiers of the U.S. Army’s 3rd Infantry Regiment (“The Old Guard”), offers a deeply moving experience, especially during the precise and solemn changing of the guard ceremony.

President John F. Kennedy’s Gravesite

Another significant highlight of the tour is the gravesite of President John F. Kennedy. Marked by the eternal flame that symbolizes his lasting impact and the nation’s enduring ideals, this peaceful setting provides an opportunity for reflection and deeper understanding of American history during the Kennedy era.

Arlington House – The Robert E. Lee Memorial

Overlooking the cemetery is Arlington House, once home to Robert E. Lee. The house provides historical context about the property’s origins and its evolution into the sacred memorial grounds we know today. Tours include exhibits exploring Lee’s complex history, the Civil War, and the significant decision to repurpose his family estate into a national cemetery.

Notable Graves and Memorials

Arlington’s grounds are also dotted with countless notable gravesites and memorials honoring veterans from every American conflict. Among these are sites commemorating the Challenger and Columbia space shuttle crews, as well as monuments dedicated to the bravery and sacrifice of soldiers from World War I, World War II, the Korean War, and Vietnam War.

Educational Value for Students and Groups

An Arlington National Cemetery tour provides students with meaningful educational experiences extending far beyond traditional classroom lessons. Visiting Arlington encourages thoughtful discussions about history, service, sacrifice, citizenship, and the lasting impact of those who dedicated their lives to the country.

Guided tours also offer critical historical insights and personal stories that bring history vividly to life. Students are prompted to reflect upon the cost of freedom and the importance of honoring those who serve.

Practical Tips for Visiting Arlington

  • Dress respectfully: Arlington is an active cemetery and a place of quiet remembrance. Visitors should maintain a respectful demeanor.

  • Comfortable footwear: Be prepared for walking on paved pathways and moderate hills.

  • Timing: Plan your visit to include witnessing the changing of the guard at the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier, occurring every hour (and every half-hour in the summer).

A tour of Arlington National Cemetery is not just a historical visit—it’s a profoundly moving experience that resonates deeply with visitors, particularly students discovering America’s past and the enduring sacrifices made by its heroes.
